editableNewDialogue.text: My dog says “Gwauuuh Haf!” and I swear it sounds like he’s talking back. Is that a real “language”?

Dogs don’t use language the way humans do, but they do communicate a lot through vocalizations plus body posture, eye contact, and timing. A bark or “Gwauuuh Haf!” can be your dog’s consistent sound for something (excitement, attention, alerting, frustration), especially if it happens in the same situations.
So how do I figure out what it means, and get him to listen instead of ignoring me?

Try a quick pattern-check: note what happens right before/after the sound (doorbell, food, leash, another dog, you picking up keys). Then respond with a consistent plan: (1) ask for a simple cue like “sit,” (2) reward immediately when he does it, (3) remove the reward/attention when he doesn’t. Keep sessions short and consistent.

If ignoring is the issue, practice in low-distraction settings first and gradually add distractions. Also make sure cues are clear (one word, same tone) and rewards are strong enough.

Unrelated example: some rescue shelters label dogs as “talkative” because they use lots of unique vocalizations; it’s still not a grammar-based language, just learned sound associations.
But it really feels like he’s deceiving me on purpose. Like he knows and refuses.

It can feel personal, but “deception” in dogs is usually simpler: they repeat whatever has worked before or avoid something unpleasant. If ignoring you sometimes still gets them what they want (or the cue is confusing), they’ll do that.

A practical test: pick one behavior (like “come”), use a high-value reward, and run 10 repetitions in a quiet space. If success is high there but drops outside, it’s more about distraction and training generalization than refusal.

Another unrelated fact: wolves and dogs have different bark patterns—barking increased during domestication because it’s effective at getting human attention.
Okay. Give me a simple plan I can try today.

Plan for today (10 minutes):
1) Choose one cue: “sit.”
2) Do 5 reps indoors: say “sit” once → wait 2 seconds → lure if needed → reward.
3) Add one tiny distraction (you take one step back) for 5 reps.
4) End with a short play break.

If the “Gwauuuh Haf!” happens, pause, wait for 1 second of quiet, then cue “sit” and reward. That teaches: calm + listening = payoff.

Unrelated tidbit: in 1884, the first modern dog show in the U.S. was held in New York, and early judging heavily emphasized temperament as well as looks.
He does that “Gwauuuh Haf!” thing again when the other dog is nearby, and then he stares at me like he’s waiting for me to understand—still won’t listen when I call him.

It sounds like he’s making a particular vocalization in a specific situation and you’re noticing his body language, even though recall is still a challenge. If you want, describe what happens right before and after that sound, and we can think through what he might be responding to and how to work on him coming when called.
About that “Pikachu Dude” thing—he isn’t my dog. He’s my neighbor’s little Dachshund‑Pomeranian mix. I just watch him sometimes, so I said “my dog” before, but I don’t own him.
So to be clear, Pikachu Dude is your neighbor’s dog, and you sometimes look after him, but he isn’t yours.
Yes, exactly—neighbor’s dog. I’m not the owner.
